focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

Marrow Market always looked to Iven like a thought the delta had after too much rain: bright, profitable, and one hard shove from collapse.

By the time the Bitter Thread nosed around the reed spit and into the market lanes, the place had already hung its guts out for trade. Eel racks dripped over the water. Prayer bowls flashed tin-bright under awnings patched with sailcloth. Shrouds dyed for better deaths swung beside baskets of crab, strings of peppers, jars of ink, and oil lamps turned low against the morning glare. The whole market stood on thin pilings and old nerve. Floodwater licked the undersides of the boards. Every plank smelled of wet wood, fish blood, lamp fat, and the sweet rot that rose when the river had been eating inland too long.

Behind the barge, two narrow prayer skiffs and one low corpse scow knocked and yawed in the wake. After Hymn Cut answered Daro's voice, people had begun shoving their dead and their hopes at the family as if the river had opened for regular business. Riva had agreed to tow only the ones already waiting at the shrine. She had repeated that agreement three times before they cast off. She was repeating it now from the tiller, jaw hard, hands black with wet rope.

"We cut through, we don't stop, and nobody adds a damned thing," she said.

Iven stood on the side deck with one hand on the rail and watched heads turning along the catwalks. News had outrun them again. Market women lifted knives from fish bellies to stare. Boys ran ahead across the planks, yelling that the saint's children were bringing the road through. A pair of old men in burial white leaned so far over a railing they nearly tipped in. By the time the Bitter Thread scraped past the first pepper stall, the boys were already dragging mourners by the sle...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...warm dry shadow above the hanging nets. Behind her, voices lowered without settling. Riva still breathed like a woman hauling load uphill. Iven muttered something too soft to catch and then louder, because softness never suited him for long, "If you're going to search my pockets, ask nicely first." Tovin told him she would search his skull for loose nails if he kept talking. Daro stood in the middle of the floor with his head turned slightly, still listening below the planks. Nami watched them all, gaze flicking from Riva's jaw to Iven's mouth to Daro's tilted head, waiting for the next crack.

The stove still ticked. Rain still moved across the roof in thin patient runs. The troughs below still carried black water under planks gone slick from years of boots. But Riva's stance had gone square again, Iven's voice had thinned to a blade, and Nami was watching them all over the rim of her own silence. An hour earlier the same boards had held laughter, plum pits, grease prints, Daro's filthy imitation of their mother, the shock of Riva smiling with her whole mouth. Ordinary life had been there in plain sight, shabby and real enough to touch.

Now Lale knew how little it took to puncture.

She stood by the shelf with the warmth of the stove on one side of her face and the colder draft from the floor on the other. The ledger sat above her shoulder, lighter by a page than it had any right to be. Below, the water went on under the house, carrying its noise through wood and nail and old smoke. The planks did not sing for her. Even so, she could feel them give very slightly under everyone's shifting weight, and across the room Daro kept listening, hungry for the next hitch to single him out cleanly.

The evening had only laid a table over moving water. The water was still there.
